text: The surgeon dissects the lymph nodes along the internal iliac vessels using robotic assistance, carefully separating them from surrounding tissues.

The surgeon resects lymph nodes along the periosteum of the sacrum, using a soft coagulation system to prevent bleeding from small blood vessels. Lymph nodes along the internal iliac vessels are dissected, revealing metastasis in the distal portion, with no invasion into the pelvic plexus.
The surgeon removes lymph nodes along the external iliac vessels, progressing the dissection towards the anterior aspect of the psoas muscle.

The surgeon removes lymph nodes along the internal obturator muscle, levator ani muscle fascia, and lumbosacral nerve, using the soft coagulation system to clot small blood vessels. The lymph nodes are then detached from the vesico-hypogastric fascia, revealing the bladder wall and bladder vessels as the dissection progresses caudally along the vesico-gastric fascia.

The surgeon follows the pubic vein through the obturator foramen to identify the obturator nerve. The obturator artery and vein are separated, clipped, and then cut, with visible precision in handling the vessels.
The surgeon completes the dissection of lymph nodes on the distal side and proceeds to the proximal side, identifying the obturator nerve at the bifurcation of the external and internal iliac veins.

The surgeon identifies the obturator artery branching from the internal iliac artery and cuts it. The internal iliac artery and vein are involved with metastatic lymph nodes, and the internal iliac artery is cut first.

The surgeon separates the internal iliac vein along the surface layer of the sacral nerve, ensuring it is safely secured due to the sparse nature of this layer. The lymph node along the sacral nerve is removed, using the soft coagulation system to clot small blood vessels without causing heat damage to the nerve.
The surgeon clips and cuts multiple inferior vesicle vessels individually before proceeding to cut the peripheral vessels adjacent to the internal iliac vein.
The surgeon divides the lymph nodes from the bladder wall, then clips and cuts the proximal side of the internal iliac vein.
The surgeon removes metastatic lymph nodes along the sacral nerve, exposing the inferior gluteal and internal pudendal arteries in the surgical field.
The surgeon clips and cuts the internal pudental artery, isolating and cutting only the proximal side after clipping.
The surgeon preserves the inferior gluteal artery, ensuring it remains intact during the procedure.
The surgeon removes the lymph nodes, including metastatic ones, as a single mass while preserving the hypogastric nerve and pelvic plexus.
